title: Pierogi z grzybami i kapustą
subtitle: Polish dumplings with mushroom and sauerkraut
excerpt: Hand-shaped dumplings with a filling of dried porcini and sauerkraut, boiled then finished in butter. The traditional Wigilia (Christmas Eve) pierogi — earthy, sour, and deeply savoury.
sourceNotes: Pierogi z grzybami i kapustą (dumplings with mushrooms and sauerkraut) are the Wigilia pierogi — served on Christmas Eve as part of the meatless twelve-dish meal. Dried forest mushrooms, particularly porcini or mixed wild mushrooms, are the correct mushroom for the filling: their concentrated, smoky flavour is what distinguishes this from an everyday mushroom dumpling. Fresh mushrooms do not produce the same result.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled, uncooked on a tray lined with baking paper. Once solid, transfer to bags. Cook from frozen, adding 2 minutes to the boiling time.
recipe.batchNotes: Make double or triple and freeze the surplus uncooked. A two-hour pierogi session is easier done in bulk.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made 2 days ahead and refrigerated. The dough is best made and used the same day.

content.text: Pierogi take an afternoon. This is not a dish to rush. The dough needs to rest; the filling needs time to cook and cool; each dumpling needs to be sealed carefully or the filling escapes into the cooking water. The payoff is substantial: a tray of finished pierogi, neatly shaped and gleaming from the butter, is one of the more satisfying things a home cook can produce.

The porcini must be soaked first, and both the mushrooms and their strained soaking liquid go into the filling. The filling must be completely cool before you fill the dumplings, or the heat will make the dough sticky and difficult to seal.

items.prepNote: soaked in 200 ml warm water for 30 minutes

content.text: Lift the soaked {{mushrooms-porcini-dried}} from the water, squeeze out excess liquid, and chop finely. Strain the soaking liquid through a fine sieve and reserve. Melt 30 g of {{unsalted-butter}} in a frying pan over medium heat. Cook the {{onion}} for 8 minutes until soft. Add the chopped mushrooms and 3 tablespoons of the soaking liquid. Cook, stirring, until the liquid has evaporated. Add the {{sauerkraut}} and cook for a further 5 minutes until the mixture is dry. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Spread onto a plate and leave to cool completely.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{eggs}}, {{soured-cream}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl until a shaggy dough forms. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth and elastic. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes at room temperature. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to 2 mm thickness. Cut rounds using an 8 cm cutter or glass. Place a teaspoon of filling in the centre of each round. Fold the dough over the filling and press the edges firmly to seal — pinch or crimp to ensure no gaps. Place on a floured tray.

Bring a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil. Cook the pierogi in batches of 8 to 10 — do not crowd the pot. They are done 2 to 3 minutes after they float to the surface. Remove with a slotted spoon. Melt the remaining {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide pan over medium heat and toss the cooked pierogi in it until lightly golden on each side. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Filling too wet, seal not tight enough, or too much filling
items.fix: The filling must be completely dry before use — if it releases water when pressed, return to the pan and cook further. Use slightly less filling per dumpling and press the seal firmly

items.cause: Not rested long enough, or worked too hard when kneading
items.fix: Leave the dough to rest a further 15 minutes wrapped. It should be soft and elastic, not stiff

items.cause: Too little mushroom soaking liquid, or sauerkraut rinsed too thoroughly
items.fix: Use the porcini soaking liquid generously — it is where most of the flavour lives. Do not rinse the sauerkraut: its acidity is part of the dish

content.text: The mushroom-and-sauerkraut filling is the Wigilia filling — it belongs to Christmas Eve in the way that mince pies belong to Christmas in Britain. The meatless filling is part of what makes it traditional, since Polish Christmas Eve is a meatless meal. These pierogi are often served with barszcz (clear red borscht), with each dumpling dropped into the bowl. At other times of year, the same dumplings appear as a starter or a main course, finished in butter and served with soured cream.
